attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

One note: `test_t3_429_not_clobbered_by_trailing_result_error` and others use `pytest.raises(ReasoningError)` where `RateLimitError`/`PermanentReasoningError` are subclasses — so `test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient` and `test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient` and `test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient` would also pass if the code mistakenly raised the *more specific* `RateLimitError` or `PermanentReasoningError`. content.text:
Confirmed hierarchy: `PermanentReasoningError` and `RateLimitError` both subclass `ReasoningError`. So the "maps to transient" tests (`test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient`, `test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient`, `test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient`) assert only `pytest.raises(ReasoningError)`, which is satisfied by ANY of the subclasses. A bug that misclassifies a generic 500 error as `PermanentReasoningError` (don't retry) or `RateLimitError` would NOT be caught — these tests are non-discriminating for the transient-vs-permanent distinction, which is the whole point of the classification. This is a genuine defect because the retry behavior depends on the exact subtype.

Let me verify the classification matters (retry logic keys on the subtype).

findings.title: High-stakes (T3) error-classification tests are non-discriminating: assert the base class ReasoningError, so a transient/permanent/rate-limit misclassification still passes
findings.description: The whole purpose of the T3 drain classifier (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:491-502) is to map an SDK result error to the CORRECT subtype, because the retry executor branches on the exact subtype: PermanentReasoningError => no retry (seam.py:599), RateLimitError => long backpressure (seam.py:601), base ReasoningError => fast backoff (seam.py:606). Both PermanentReasoningError and RateLimitError subclass ReasoningError (seam.py:66,70,74). Three drain tests assert the classification but only via pytest.raises(ReasoningError) (the base class): test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient (line 143-146), test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient (a 500, line 168-171), and test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient (line 202-206). Because pytest.raises catches subclasses, these pass even if the code misclassifies — e.g. a regression that mapped a generic 500 to PermanentReasoningError (don't retry) or RateLimitError would still satisfy pytest.raises(ReasoningError). The transient-vs-permanent distinction these tests claim to guard is exactly what is NOT asserted, so reverting the 'fall through to base ReasoningError' branch to a permanent/rate-limit classification would not be caught. By contrast, the bulk-path classification tests in test_reasoning.py (e.g. test_bulk_complete_classifies_404_as_permanent_by_status:382, test_malformed_params_raise_typed_permanent_error:404) correctly assert the SPECIFIC subtype, confirming this is an isolated weakness rather than an intentional convention. The empty-reply and mid-stream-raise cases are genuinely meant to be the base/transient type (retryable), so the assertion should pin that they are NOT the more-specific subtypes.
findings.poc: In src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py replace the generic fall-through at line 498 (`raise ReasoningError(...)`) with `raise PermanentReasoningError(...)`. This is a real misclassification: a transient 500 would now be treated as non-retryable. Re-run tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py::test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient — it still PASSES because PermanentReasoningError is a subclass of ReasoningError. The test fails to detect that the 500 is no longer retried.
findings.suggested_fix:
Make the transient assertions discriminating by excluding the more-specific subtypes. For each of the three tests, after capturing the exception assert the exact transient type, e.g.:

    with pytest.raises(ReasoningError) as ei:
        _run()
    assert type(ei.value) is ReasoningError  # exactly transient, not Permanent/RateLimit
    assert not isinstance(ei.value, (PermanentReasoningError, RateLimitError))

(or add a dedicated TransientReasoningError subclass and assert it). This makes a 500->permanent or empty->permanent regression fail every run, matching the discrimination already used on the bulk path.

I analyzed all 30 test files under /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests for the requested anti-patterns.

What I checked and found clean:
- Swallowed exceptions in unit tests: none (no `except ... : pass`).
- Probabilistic/seed/hash/order flakiness: the `random.Random(7)` resample test (test_confirm.py:205) and the seed-independence guard (test_immutability.py:56) are correctly deterministic — the latter even runs in a subprocess with pinned `PYTHONHASHSEED=2` and has an honest comment distinguishing the illustrative in-process check from the discriminating one.
- Docs-drift guards (test_docs_drift.py): well-constructed, pin exact localized strings and explicitly defend against loose-substring escapes.
- `pass`-only blocks in test_pipeline.py / test_observability.py: legitimate no-op stub methods on fake trackers, not vacuous asserts.
- Integration `except Exception` blocks: mostly defensible (skip on live-infra flakiness). Note: test_persistence_live.py:58-68 wraps assertions inside a `try/except Exception: pytest.skip`, so a real nearest-neighbor correctness failure becomes a skip — a minor masking risk, but it's a skif-gated live test, so I did not raise it as a primary finding.

One genuine reproducible defect (medium): The high-stakes (T3) error-classification tests in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py assert only the base class `ReasoningError`, which catches its `PermanentReasoningError` and `RateLimitError` subclasses. Since the retry executor branches on the exact subtype (seam.py:599-606), these "maps to transient" tests are non-discriminating — a misclassification of a generic 500/empty/mid-stream-error as permanent or rate-limit would not be caught, even though that breaks the retry semantics the tests claim to guard. The bulk path (test_reasoning.py:382, :404) correctly asserts the specific subtype, confirming this is an isolated gap. Full repro and fix are in the structured finding.
